task: I’m drafting a short reply to a customer complaint where two people are giving different accounts of what happened, and I want it to be clear and professional. Can you help me word it so it asks for the needed details without overcommitting to any conclusion yet?

prefs.explanation: He is likely most comfortable with a straightforward, courteous tone rather than overly casual banter. His work style centers on reliable service and steady standards, so a professional register fits how he seems to want to be treated. He probably does not want exaggerated friendliness that feels performative.

prefs.short: Wants enough detail to act, not rambling
prefs.explanation: He seems to prefer practical responses that get to the point while still covering what matters. His background suggests he values clear status, concrete next steps, and enough explanation to avoid confusion. Long, wandering answers would likely feel inefficient to him.

He appears to respond best to direct communication rather than hedged or overly cautious phrasing. In his work, specific updates and plain responsibility matter more than soft language. He likely appreciates candor as long as it stays respectful.

He seems to do well with structured information that can be scanned quickly and acted on. Given his process-oriented style, bullets, steps, and concise summaries would probably feel more useful than dense prose. He likely wants help that is easy to verify and follow.

He likely does not mind being told when something is off, as long as it is grounded and respectful. His fairness-and-accountability mindset suggests he values honest correction over polite agreement. He probably prefers a helper who flags risks clearly instead of smoothing them over.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it neutral, acknowledge the discrepancy, and ask for the specific details you need.

### Simple structure
1. Acknowledge the complaint
2. Note that the accounts don’t match
3. Say you’re reviewing the records
4. Ask for the missing detail(s)
5. Avoid saying who is right yet

### Example reply
> Hi [Name],  
> Thanks for bringing this to our attention. We’re reviewing the records, and at this point we have two different accounts of what happened. To make sure we handle this correctly, could you please send any details you have on [date/time/item/location]?  
>  
> Once we have that, we’ll compare it with our records and follow up with the next step.  

### Slightly firmer version
> Hi [Name],  
> We’re looking into this, but the current accounts don’t line up yet. To verify what happened, please send any supporting details you have, including [specific info]. We’ll review that against our records and get back to you with what we can confirm.  

### Good rules
- Don’t pick a side yet
- Don’t say “we agree” or “you’re correct” before checking
- Ask for one or two specific details, not a long list
- Keep the next step clear

If you want, I can make it sound more formal, more direct, or more customer-friendly.

per_pref.reason: Uses a courteous, professional tone like “Thanks for bringing this to our attention” and “we’ll review the records,” without being overly casual.
Gives enough detail to act: a structure, two example replies, and concrete guidance like asking for “[date/time/item/location]” and “one or two specific details.”
Very direct and straightforward: “keep it neutral,” “the accounts don’t match,” “don’t pick a side yet,” and “ask for the missing detail(s).”
Strongly organized with bullets, numbered steps, and labeled sections like “Simple structure,” “Example reply,” and “Good rules,” making it easy to scan and use.
Includes firm corrective guidance such as “Don’t pick a side yet” and “Don’t say ‘we agree’ or ‘you’re correct’ before checking,” which shows clear pushback when needed.
